items.title: BRCA and ovarian cancer risk
items.contentHtml:
Risk factor: BRCA mutations

  • BRCA1/BRCA2 mutations confer cumulative risk of ovarian cancer of 39-46% (BRCA1) and 12-20% (BRCA2) at age 70.
  • Pathophysiology: loss of DNA homologous recombination repair, accumulation of genomic aberrations.
  • The Molecular profile includes genomic instability and high frequency of mutation in TP53.
  • It is associated with high-grade serous carcinoma; The presence of a BRCA germline mutation is an indication for prophylactic salpingo-oophorectomy.

items.title: Most common clinical presentation
items.contentHtml:
Most common symptom: abdominal distension

  • Abdominal distension (70-80%) is the most common symptom, followed by pelvic pain, early satiety and urinary urgency.
  • The classic triad of abdominal distention, pelvic mass and ascites points to advanced ovarian cancer.
  • Symptoms are usually non-specific and of onset. insidious; The average diagnostic delay is 6-12 months.
  • In early stages it can be asymptomatic; incidental finding is rare.

items.title: CA-125 in ovarian cancer
items.contentHtml:
Tumor marker: CA-125

  • CA-125 (carbohydrate antigen 125) is a surface glycoprotein elevated in >80% of serous ovarian epithelial carcinomas.
  • It is not specific: it can be elevated in endometriosis, pelvic inflammatory disease, pregnancy, cirrhosis and other tumors.
  • Normal value <35 U/mL; levels >200 U/mL are highly suggestive of malignancy in postmenopausal women.
  • It is used to monitor response to treatment and detect recurrence; It is not recommended as population screening.

items.title: Image in ovarian cancer
items.contentHtml:
Imaging finding: complex solid-cystic mass

  • Complex solid-cystic mass with papillary components, thick septa (>3 mm) and areas of necrosis is the typical ultrasound finding of ovarian cancer.
  • The IOTA (International Ovarian Tumor Analysis) classification defines malignancy criteria: presence of complex solid-cystic mass, ascites, flow High-speed central Doppler.
  • Contrast-enhanced MRI shows heterogeneous enhancement of the solid components.
  • Computed tomography evaluates peritoneal extension, omental implants and retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy.

items.title: Laparotomy in staging
items.contentHtml:
Mandatory surgical staging: laparotomy

  • Laparotomy is the procedure of choice for surgical staging of ovarian cancer; includes peritoneal cytology, inspection of the entire cavity, random peritoneal biopsies and omentectomy.
  • Staging is performed according to the FIGO (International Federation of Gynecology and Obstetrics) classification: stage I limited to ovaries, stage II pelvic extension, stage III peritoneal or lymph node dissemination, stage IV distant metastasis.
  • Surgery must be performed by a gynecological oncologist; optimal cytoreduction (residual <1 cm) improves survival.
  • Laparoscopy has limitations for complete staging and cytoreduction.

items.foco: Germ cell tumor: elevated AFP and beta-hCG
items.title: Markers in germ cell tumors
items.contentHtml:
Germ cell tumor: elevated AFP and beta-hCG

  • Germ cell tumors produce specific markers: elevated alpha-fetoprotein (AFP) in endodermal sinus tumor and immature teratoma; beta human chorionic gonadotropin (beta-hCG) elevated in choriocarcinoma and dysgerminoma (syncytiotrophoblast).
  • Pure dysgerminoma elevates lactate dehydrogenase (LDH) and can elevate beta-hCG if it contains syncytiotrophoblastic cells.
  • These markers are used for diagnosis, monitoring, and detection of recurrence; They must normalize after chemotherapy.
  • They are chemosensitive tumors; Treatment includes conservative surgery and chemotherapy with bleomycin, etoposide, and cisplatin (BEP).

items.title: Granulosa cell tumor
items.contentHtml:
Stroma tumors: granulosa cell tumors

  • Granulosa cell tumor is the most common stromal tumor; It originates from the granulosa of the ovarian follicle.
  • Adult (95%) and juvenile subtype; produces estrogen, causing abnormal uterine bleeding, precocious puberty or endometrial hyperplasia.
  • It is associated with a mutation in the FOXL2 gene (adult subtype), detectable by immunohistochemistry (inhibin, calretinin positive).
  • It is low grade, but can relapse late (>10 years); The treatment is surgical (staging) and does not require adjuvant chemotherapy in the initial stages.

items.title: Radical surgery in ovarian cancer
items.contentHtml:
Surgical treatment: hysterectomy + salpingo-oophorectomy

  • Standard surgical treatment for advanced epithelial ovarian cancer includes total abdominal hysterectomy, bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy, omentectomy and maximal cytoreduction.
  • In low-grade early stages (IA, IB), conservative surgery (unilateral salpingo-oophorectomy) may be considered if fertility preservation is desired.
  • The Total abdominal hysterectomy is performed to eliminate microscopic disease in the uterus and ligaments.
  • Optimal cytoreduction (residual disease <1 cm) is the most important prognostic factor.

items.title: First-line chemotherapy regimen
items.contentHtml:
Adjuvant chemotherapy: carboplatin + paclitaxel

  • The standard first-line regimen in advanced epithelial ovarian cancer is carboplatin (area under the curve 5-6) plus paclitaxel (175 mg/m²) every 3 weeks, for 6 cycles.
  • Carboplatin is a platinum derivative. that forms DNA adducts; paclitaxel stabilizes microtubules and inhibits mitosis.
  • In patients with a BRCA mutation, a poly(ADP-ribose) polymerase (PARP) inhibitor can be added as maintenance (olaparib, niraparib).
  • Intraperitoneal administration of chemotherapy (cisplatin + paclitaxel) is reserved for optimal cytoreduction.

items.title: Most frequent histological subtype
items.contentHtml:
Epithelial ovarian cancer: high-grade serous

  • High-grade serous carcinoma represents 70% of epithelial ovarian cancers; It originates in the tubal fimbria (tubal serosa theory).
  • It is characterized by mutation in TP53 (>95%), genomic instability and high expression of Ki-67.
  • It occurs in advanced stages (III/IV) with peritoneal dissemination; 5-year survival is 30-40%.
  • The diagnosis is based on histology with papillary pattern, psamomas and marked nuclear atypia; Immunohistochemistry: cytokeratin 7 positive, cytokeratin 20 negative, PAX8 positive.

items.title: Meigs syndrome
items.contentHtml:
Meigs syndrome: ascites + pleural effusion

  • The Meigs syndrome is defined by the triad: benign ovarian tumor (fibroma, fibrothecoma), ascites and pleural effusion (generally right), which resolve after resection of the tumor.
  • Pathophysiology: the tumor produces cytokines (vascular endothelial growth factor) that increase peritoneal and pleural permeability, causing transudate.
  • Pleural effusion is usually unilateral and transudate type; ascites is also transudate.
  • It is crucial to differentiate it from metastatic ovarian cancer: in Meigs syndrome the tumor is benign and there are no peritoneal implants.

items.title: Surgical prophylaxis in BRCA
items.contentHtml:
Prevention: prophylactic salpingo-oophorectomy in BRCA

  • Bilateral prophylactic salpingo-oophorectomy reduces the risk of ovarian cancer by 80-96% in carriers of BRCA1/BRCA2 mutations.
  • It is recommended to perform it between 35-40 years of age for BRCA1 and 40-45 years of age for BRCA2, after completing parity.
  • The entire surgical specimen must be examined (SEE-FIM protocol) to detect occult tubal serous carcinoma (precursor).
  • Bilateral prophylactic salpingo-oophorectomy also reduces the risk of breast cancer in BRCA1/BRCA2 carriers if performed before menopause.
